explanation: The procedure sets NOCOUNT OFF and never sets NOCOUNT ON, so every statement sends row-count messages to the client, adding network and client noise.
XACT_ABORT is set OFF while the procedure later uses explicit transactions (lines 182, 239); a run-time error can leave a transaction open or partially applied.
SELECT * on Sales.Orders couples callers to every current and future column and pulls unnecessary data such as the nvarchar(MAX) comment columns.
SELECT TOP (10) from Sales.Orders has no ORDER BY, so the returned orders are arbitrary and can vary between executions.
DISTINCT on Sales.Orders.OrderID is redundant because OrderID is the primary key; the duplicate-elimination step is pure overhead.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, duplicated, or missing rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) applies a function to the filtered column, forcing a scan; rewrite as a range predicate (>= '2016-01-01' AND < '2017-01-01') to allow a seek.
Sales.Orders.CustomerID (int) is compared to @CustomerIdText (nvarchar), forcing implicit conversion of the column side and blocking seeks on FK_Sales_Orders_CustomerID.
(@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID) defeats cardinality estimation and index seeking; use separate statements or OPTION (RECOMPILE).
LIKE N'%urgent%' on Sales.Orders.Comments starts with %, so no index seek is possible.
NOT IN with a subquery returns zero rows if the subquery ever yields a NULL; NOT EXISTS expresses the same logic safely and usually performs better.
Wrapping o.CustomerID in ISNULL makes the predicate non-sargable; CustomerID is NOT NULL, so the ISNULL wrapper is unnecessary anyway.
o.OrderID + 0 forces row-by-row evaluation of the primary key and prevents direct index matching.
o.CustomerID <> 1 is a negative predicate that typically scans the index instead of seeking.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers multiplies every order by every customer with no join predicate.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary order/customer pair.
Comma-separated FROM (Sales.Orders, Sales.OrderLines) with the join condition in WHERE is legacy syntax and makes accidental cartesian products easier.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary order row.
The COUNT(*) subquery on Sales.OrderLines executes once per qualifying order row; a LEFT JOIN with GROUP BY computes all line counts in one pass.
The correlated EXISTS on Sales.OrderLines runs per outer order row; expressing it as a set-based semi-join gives the optimizer more freedom.
The CASE WHEN o.Comments IS NULL THEN 0 ELSE 1 END expression is duplicated in the select list and ORDER BY; order by the HasComments alias instead.
ORDER BY 2 references a select-list position; reordering the select list silently changes the sort column.
OFFSET/FETCH paginates on OrderDate alone, which is not unique; add a tie-breaker such as OrderID for stable pages.
The branches are disjoint by construction (OrderID = 1 vs OrderID = 2), so UNION's duplicate removal is wasted work; use UNION ALL.
(SELECT COUNT(*) ...) > 0 counts every matching order line when only existence matters; use EXISTS so the scan can stop at the first row.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID FROM Sales.Orders silently accepts multiple rows and keeps an arbitrary last value; use a deterministic TOP 1 with ORDER BY.
Table variable @OrderIds carries no statistics and gets a one-row estimate on older compatibility levels; a temp table is safer once it feeds joins.
INSERT ... SELECT TOP (5) without ORDER BY inserts an arbitrary set of order ids into @OrderIds.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders derives the temp table schema implicitly; declaring the table explicitly gives control over types, nullability, and indexes.
TOP (5) without ORDER BY selects an arbitrary set of orders into #ImplicitOrders.
Cursor order_cursor processes orders row by row for work that can be expressed as a set-based operation.
The cursor's SELECT TOP (2) has no ORDER BY, so it iterates an arbitrary pair of orders.
The WHILE loop performs iterative work that should be expressed as a set-based operation.
@CustomerID is concatenated into the SQL string; bind it as a typed parameter to sp_executesql instead.
EXEC (@Sql) runs concatenated SQL with no parameter binding and no chance of safe plan reuse.
@OrderID is concatenated into the SQL string instead of being passed as a typed parameter.
sp_executesql is called without parameter definitions even though the query consumes @OrderID, defeating the purpose of parameterized dynamic SQL.
#Scratch is created but never dropped before the procedure ends.
The CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status, so callers observe success after an error.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; THROW is the modern syntax and preserves the original error context.
PRINT output is buffered and unstructured, making it unsuitable for diagnostics; use logging or THROW.
CustomerID = 1 embeds a business identifier directly in the query instead of using the @CustomerID parameter.
The unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded in the OrderDate predicate; pass it as a parameter.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') without a style parameter depends on session DATEFORMAT (January 2 vs February 1).
COALESCE on both sides of the join predicate between Orders and Invoices inhibits normal join optimization and index usage.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary matching order.
Sales.Orders is self-joined on its primary key only to return the same OrderID already available in the row; the join adds nothing.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary order.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts the entire Orders table by an unselective nvarchar(MAX) column, an expensive wide sort.
SUM over all of Sales.OrderLines aggregates the whole fact-like table for one scalar result with no restrictive filter.
HAVING ol.OrderID > 0 is a row-level predicate applied after grouping; move it to WHERE so rows are eliminated before aggregation.
COUNT(*) without GROUP BY always returns exactly one row, so ORDER BY OrderCount is unnecessary.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; use separate UPDATE/INSERT statements with appropriate locking instead.
SET target.Comments = target.Comments writes the existing value, generating logging and locking with no data change.
UPDATE Sales.Orders SET Comments = Comments changes nothing but still incurs write logging and row locks.
DELETE from Sales.OrderLines filtered only on Quantity = -1 uses a broad non-key predicate that can remove many unrelated rows.
WAITFOR DELAY intentionally blocks a worker thread inside the engine; use external asynchronous scheduling instead.
BEGIN TRANSACTION runs outside a TRY/CATCH boundary, so an error can leave the transaction open.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT Sales.Orders ON changes session state and couples the procedure to fragile identity management.
EXEC sp_who is invoked without a schema qualifier, adding resolution ambiguity and a maintenance hazard.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 limits all subsequent statements in the session and is never restored to 0.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (...) has no explicit column list, so it breaks or misplaces data when the table definition changes.
The category id 99 is a magic value embedded in the INSERT; derive it or accept it as a parameter.
INSERT INTO #DemoCustomers SELECT ... has no explicit column list, relying on column order.
LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' starts with a wildcard, so the unique index on CustomerName cannot be sought.
@CustomerName is concatenated directly into the dynamic SQL string.
User-supplied @CustomerName is embedded in executed SQL text; a value containing a quote breaks out of the LIKE literal and alters the statement (SQL injection). Bind it via sp_executesql parameters.
The generated LIKE pattern starts with %, forcing a scan of #DemoCustomers on every execution.
sp_executesql is invoked with no parameter list even though the query consumes @CustomerName.
sp_executesql is called without the sys schema qualifier.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers has no WHERE clause, so CustomerCategoryID is overwritten on every row.
CustomerCategoryID = 1 embeds a magic business identifier instead of using a parameter.
The WHERE predicate on o.OrderDate references the LEFT JOINed Sales.Orders, silently converting the outer join into an inner join; move it to the ON clause.
The date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded in the join filter; parameterize it.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL never evaluates to true, so this query always returns zero rows; use IS NULL.
dbo.GetCustomerType is a scalar UDF invoked per row in the SELECT list, forcing row-by-row evaluation and blocking parallelism.
The CATCH block follows BEGIN TRANSACTION but never issues ROLLBACK, leaving the transaction open after an error.
The CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and never rethrows, so callers cannot detect the failure.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hard-codes an index choice on Sales.Customers, overriding the optimizer and breaking if index 1 is rebuilt, renamed, or dropped.
Four levels of nested derived tables (t1/t2/t3) add no logic; collapse them into a single query or a CTE.
LIKE '%A%' on CustomerName cannot use an index seek.
SELECT * from the TVF returns every column whether the caller needs it or not.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement table-valued function; an inline TVF would be folded into the outer query and optimized far better.
